sections.content: Overview

Training data is the foundational fuel that determines everything about a Large Language Model—its capabilities, limitations, biases, and real-world performance. Without careful consideration of dataset composition, size, quality, and preprocessing, even a perfectly architected transformer will learn spurious patterns, amplify harmful stereotypes, develop incorrect reasoning habits, or fail catastrophically on edge cases. The stakes are profound: GPT-4, Claude, and LLaMA were trained on terabytes of text curated through meticulous filtering, deduplication, and quality gates that took months to develop. A single data issue—undetected bias in training corpora, data leakage between train/test splits, or inclusion of personally identifiable information—can compromise model safety, legal compliance, and public trust. This lesson explores the technical and practical dimensions of dataset construction, cleaning, sampling strategies, and the downstream effects of these decisions on model behavior. Understanding why training data matters is not optional; it is fundamental to building language models that are both capable and trustworthy.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Rahul Dravid is building a batting academy to teach young cricketers the fundamentals. The quality and composition of training scenarios he exposes his students to—whether they face fast bowlers from flat pitches, spinners from rough surfaces, yorkers at death overs, or a balanced diet of all conditions—directly determines what kind of batsmen they become. If Dravid only trains players against medium-pace bowling on perfect wickets (biased data), his students will have a catastrophic failure rate against Jasprit Bumrah's pace or Ravi Ashwin's spin. The analogy maps perfectly: the academy's training curriculum is the dataset, the variety and balance of bowling conditions represent dataset diversity and distribution, and the students' performance in real matches reflects the model's generalization. Just as Dravid must ensure training covers all match conditions—powerplays, middle overs, death bowling, various grounds—an LLM trainer must ensure their dataset spans diverse domains, writing styles, reasoning patterns, and edge cases. The profound insight is that a model is imprisoned by its training data; it cannot learn what it has never seen, and it will over-weight patterns it sees repeatedly. This is why careful curation is not a luxury—it is an absolute necessity.
Core Concepts
Dataset Composition and Source Selection

Dataset composition refers to the strategic selection and mixing of text sources that form the training corpus. Large-scale LLMs typically integrate data from dozens of sources: web crawls (Common Crawl), books (Project Gutenberg, LibriVox), academic papers (arXiv, Papers with Code repositories), code repositories (GitHub), and curated conversational data. The critical decision is how much weight to assign each source. For instance, if code capability is important, GitHub content might represent 10-20% of training tokens; if reasoning is paramount, academic papers receive higher weight despite their smaller volume. Source selection directly influences the model's implicit world knowledge, coding ability, writing style, and reasoning patterns. Poor composition decisions lead to models that are simultaneously knowledgeable about obscure Wikipedia trivia yet unable to answer basic math questions, or fluent in formal academic writing but helpless in casual conversation. The composition also determines which biases will be present—news sources introduce recency bias and editorial perspective, social media introduces informal language and community-specific slang, while academic sources introduce credentialism and technical jargon. Each source carries its own distributional assumptions about what the world looks like.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: When the Indian national cricket team selects bowling combinations for a Test series, the coach must balance fast bowlers, spinners, and all-rounders in the squad. If the squad consists exclusively of fast bowlers (like picking only short-form cricket players for a Test), the team will be helpless against spin-heavy pitches in Asia. Virat Kohli and Rohit Sharma expect their support cast to match the expected conditions: spinner-friendly pitches in India require Ravichandran Ashwin and Axar Patel in the playing XI. The dataset composition is precisely this strategic squad selection: each source type is a bowling discipline. Web crawls provide breadth like fast bowling—quick coverage, high volume, but prone to noise; academic papers are like spin bowling—slower, more complex, but invaluable in specific conditions; code repositories are like all-rounders—specialized yet versatile. Just as a badly balanced squad (all fast bowlers) fails regardless of individual talent, a dataset skewed toward one source type (say, only Reddit posts) will produce a model that excels in casual conversation but fails at technical reasoning. The insight is that diversity in dataset composition is not aesthetic—it is functional. The model learns to weight different reasoning modes, writing styles, and knowledge domains proportionally to their training representation.
Data Cleaning and Filtering

Data cleaning and filtering involve removing or correcting corrupted, irrelevant, or harmful data before training begins. Raw internet text contains massive amounts of noise: truncated HTML, duplicate content, non-text artifacts, low-quality spam, and personally identifiable information (PII) like credit card numbers, email addresses, and social security numbers. Filtering requires rule-based heuristics, machine learning classifiers, and manual inspection. For example, Common Crawl—a massive web archive containing petabytes of text—is typically filtered to remove pages with excessive boilerplate markup, low language quality scores, copyright-flagged content, and content flagged by toxicity classifiers. Deduplication is critical: without it, models can memorize the exact same paragraph repeated thousands of times across the internet, leading to inflated confidence on duplicated content and potential copyright violations. The filtering process is inherently lossy—every filter removes signal alongside noise. A toxicity filter that is too aggressive removes discussions about difficult historical or social topics; a deduplication threshold that is too strict removes legitimate variance in how similar ideas are expressed. The challenge is calibrating these trade-offs. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Meta employ teams of data engineers and policy specialists to design filtering pipelines that balance safety, diversity, and informativeness. Empirically, roughly 80% of raw internet text is discarded in modern LLM training pipelines.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: When match officials review footage after a cricket match to identify controversial moments—such as analyzing whether MS Dhoni's caught dismissal was clean or if the ball had pitched outside leg stump using DRS technology—they must filter signal from noise. The footage contains thousands of frames, camera angles, and potential artifacts. Officials apply strict filtering criteria: they discard irrelevant angles, enhance slow-motion shots, and focus on the critical moment. If they apply filters incorrectly—say, discarding all footage that shows even momentary doubt—they miss legitimate uncertainty; if they apply filters too loosely and admit all camera artifacts and production flubs as evidence, they introduce noise that confuses the decision. The dataset filtering process mirrors this: raw crawled data is the unfiltered match footage, containing garbage (corrupted encoding, spam, PII), duplicates (the same moment filmed from multiple angles), and harmful content (discussions that should not influence decision-making). Filtering rules are like the DRS criteria: they aim to keep authentic signal while removing artifacts. The critical parallel is that over-filtering removes legitimate edge cases (just as rejecting all inconclusive DRS footage loses important context), while under-filtering lets noise dominate. The insight is that filtering is not a simple on/off decision but a calibration problem. The team designing the filter must understand what content matters for the downstream task and what truly harms model behavior.
Data Distribution and Sampling Strategies

Data distribution describes the frequency and probability with which different content types appear in the training dataset. Natural distribution on the internet is heavily skewed: English content vastly outnumbers other languages (roughly 60% of Common Crawl is English despite English being spoken by only ~15% of humans), recent content vastly outnumbers historical content, and popular topics vastly outnumber niche topics. Training a model on this natural distribution creates an English-biased, recency-biased model that under-represents minority languages, historical knowledge, and specialized domains. Sampling strategies address this through intentional reweighting. Upsampling means increasing the proportion of underrepresented data; downsampling means decreasing the proportion of overrepresented data. For example, Chinook, a recent ORCA model, intentionally overweights reasoning-heavy content (math, code, scientific papers) and underweights social media and web boilerplate relative to their natural frequency. The epoch count also matters—models typically see repeated shuffles through the dataset over multiple training epochs, allowing important-but-rare data types to appear more often. The trade-off is that aggressive reweighting can make the model unrepresentative of how language actually appears in the wild. If you double-weight code but code represents 2% of internet text, the model will be artificially fluent in coding tasks but hallucinate code-like thinking when solving non-programming problems. The empirical finding is that careful sampling choices can improve downstream task performance by 10-30%, but careless reweighting harms model coherence and generalization.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider how the International Cricket Council (ICC) designs tournament schedules to determine world rankings. If rankings were based purely on the natural distribution of cricket played globally, the top teams would be those that play the most matches (India, Australia, England with their IPL, Big Bash, and Hundred franchises). This creates a biased world ranking that overweights franchise cricket and underweights international Test cricket. To fix this, the ICC intentionally upsamples Test matches and international competitions in the ranking calculation, giving more weight per match to rare but important cricket formats. Similarly, the ICC downsamples franchise cricket because it is naturally overrepresented. Virat Kohli might play 50 IPL matches per year but only 5-10 Test matches; without reweighting, his IPL performances would dominate his ranking. The dataset distribution of cricket is like raw training data—biased toward high-volume, low-value content. Sampling strategies are like the ICC's intentional reweighting—deliberately increasing the importance of rare, high-signal content (Test cricket) and decreasing noise (franchise clutter). The insight is that natural data frequency does not reflect importance or value. Models must be actively corrected toward the distributions that matter for real-world utility, not left to passively absorb internet statistics.
Bias Detection and Mitigation

Bias in training data refers to systematic over- or under-representation of certain groups, perspectives, or concepts that causes the model to produce unfair, inaccurate, or harmful outputs. Unlike statistical noise, bias is directional: a biased model is consistently wrong in a particular direction. Common sources include gender bias (female occupations appear less frequently with competence markers in text), racial bias (minority groups are over-associated with negative descriptors), and geographic bias (non-Western countries are under-represented in English text). Detection requires both quantitative metrics and qualitative auditing. Quantitative approaches include measuring representation (how many sentences discuss women as engineers?) and measuring association (when the model encounters 'engineer,' what is the probability it next generates a gendered pronoun?). Qualitative auditing involves hiring annotators to review model outputs on sensitive topics and flag harmful stereotypes. Mitigation strategies include data augmentation (synthetically generating underrepresented perspectives), balanced sampling (ensuring equal representation of groups), and adversarial debiasing (training models to perform equally well across demographic groups). However, bias mitigation is fundamentally constrained by the data available. If source texts reflect historical biases—such as women being underrepresented in medical textbooks published before 1990—debiasing those sources without synthetic augmentation is impossible. The hard truth is that some bias is baked into the historical record, and models faithfully represent that bias unless explicitly overridden.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: When the IPL auction house selects teams for franchises, if the auction naturally produces teams where overseas players are predominantly fast bowlers and Indian spinners are predominantly from a single region, the resulting league exhibits systematic bias. A team that over-recruits overseas batsmen but only Indian bowlers will have structural imbalance that affects match outcomes. To detect this bias, the IPL administrator might measure: What percentage of overseas players are fast bowlers? (quantitative). What is the success rate of overseas batsmen versus Indian batsmen in our league? (association). When overseas fast bowler Jasprit Bumrah was selected by Mumbai Indians early, did team dynamics and selection patterns change league-wide? (qualitative audit). To mitigate, the IPL might impose caps: no more than 60% of overseas slots can be filled by fast bowlers, or ensure each region of India is represented proportionally in the spinner pool. However, the historical record of cricket shows that certain regions have traditionally produced more spinners—bias is not invented but inherited from cricket history. No auction rule can change the fact that Rajasthan's pitches historically developed spinners and pace bowlers differently. The insight is that bias in datasets mirrors league-wide systemic bias: it is detected through measurement, mitigated through explicit policy overrides, but ultimately constrained by the historical record that generated the data.
Data Versioning and Reproducibility

Data versioning involves maintaining precise records of which data sources, preprocessing steps, and filtering rules were used to create a training corpus, allowing future researchers to reproduce training runs and understand model behavior. Without versioning, a researcher cannot distinguish whether a model's poor performance on a downstream task is due to architecture changes, hyperparameter tuning, or upstream data pipeline modifications. Industrial language model training involves hundreds of preprocessing decisions—which CommonCrawl snapshots were used? What version of the deduplication algorithm? Which toxicity classifier? How were edge cases handled? When a model is deployed and later found to behave unexpectedly, engineers must trace the issue back through the data pipeline. This tracing is only possible with comprehensive versioning. Reproducibility is also critical for scientific rigor: other researchers should be able to inspect the exact dataset and reproduce the training process. However, reproducibility in LLM training is complicated by legal restrictions—much training data involves copyrighted material, and sharing entire training datasets is legally and practically infeasible. Most major labs publish detailed dataset documentation (cards) describing their data sources, filtering procedures, and any known issues, but rarely release the full dataset. The practical standard is that researchers should document their pipelines thoroughly enough that a competent engineer could reconstruct a similar dataset using the same sources and procedures, even if the exact bytes are not identical.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: When a cricket team analyzes Rohit Sharma's batting performance across multiple seasons, they must maintain detailed records: What were the pitch conditions in each match (hard, dusty, wet)? What was the opposition bowling attack? What was Rohit's fitness level and form at the time? Without these records—the 'versioning' of conditions—a coach cannot distinguish whether Rohit's recent batting slump is due to a technical flaw in his technique, inadequate preparation, or simply facing world-class bowlers. If a coach tries to reproduce Rohit's success from 2019 (a banner year), the coach needs to know exactly what conditions, opposition, and team dynamics existed then. Duckworth-Lewis calculations in rain-affected matches require precise versioning of when rain occurred, weather conditions, and overs bowled. The dataset versioning in LLM training is exactly this: maintaining meticulous records of data sources (like recording pitch conditions), preprocessing (like fitness levels), and filtering rules (like opposition strength). When a deployed model behaves unexpectedly, engineers trace the issue by consulting version history—'was this data source included? Which toxicity filter was active?'—just as Rohit's coach consults match records. The insight is that reproducibility is not academic luxury but operational necessity: without it, teams cannot diagnose problems, improve systems, or communicate findings to stakeholders.

import hashlib
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Dict
from collections import defaultdict
import random
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class TrainingMatch:
    """Represents a training dataset as a cricket match."""
    match_id: str
    player_name: str
    innings_number: int
    runs_scored: int
    balls_faced: int
    source_type: str  # 'web_crawl', 'academic', 'code', 'social_media'
    quality_score: float  # 0.0 to 1.0
    is_duplicate: bool
    contains_pii: bool
    bias_category: str  # 'gender', 'geographic', 'temporal', 'none'
    
    def calculate_batting_average(self) -> float:
        """Calculate runs per ball (model performance proxy)."""
        return self.runs_scored / max(self.balls_faced, 1)

class CricketDatasetPipeline:
    """Simulates the complete dataset pipeline for LLM training."""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.raw_data: List[TrainingMatch] = []
        self.cleaned_data: List[TrainingMatch] = []
        self.deduplicated_data: List[TrainingMatch] = []
        self.balanced_data: List[TrainingMatch] = []
        self.statistics = {}
        
    def ingest_raw_data(self
sections.matches:
List[TrainingMatch]):
        """STEP 1: Ingest raw data from multiple sources."""
        self.raw_data = matches
        print(f"[INGEST] Loaded {len(self.raw_data)} raw training examples")
        
    def filter_data_quality(self
sections.min_quality:
float = 0.5) -> List[TrainingMatch]:
        """STEP 2: Filter out low-quality and harmful data."""
        before_count = len(self.raw_data)
        
        filtered = []
        pii_removed = 0
        low_quality_removed = 0
        
        for match in self.raw_data:
            # Remove PII (like filtering passwords from raw text)
            if match.contains_pii:
                pii_removed += 1
                continue
                
            # Remove low-quality content
            if match.quality_score < min_quality:
                low_quality_removed += 1
                continue
                
            filtered.append(match)
        
        self.cleaned_data = filtered
        removal_rate = (before_count - len(self.cleaned_data)) / before_count * 100
        print(f"[FILTER] Removed {pii_removed} PII-containing entries")
        print(f"[FILTER] Removed {low_quality_removed} low-quality entries")
        print(f"[FILTER] Total removal rate: {removal_rate:.1f}%")
        return self.cleaned_data
        
    def deduplicate_data(self) -> List[TrainingMatch]:
        """STEP 3: Remove duplicate training examples."""
        seen_hashes = {}
        deduplicated = []
        duplicates_found = 0
        
        for match in self.cleaned_data:
            # Create hash of significant features
            data_hash = hashlib.md5(
                f"{match.player_name}{match.runs_scored}{match.balls_faced}".encode()
            ).hexdigest()
            
            if data_hash not in seen_hashes:
                seen_hashes[data_hash] = match
                deduplicated.append(match)
            else:
                duplicates_found += 1
        
        self.deduplicated_data = deduplicated
        dup_rate = duplicates_found / len(self.cleaned_data) * 100
        print(f"[DEDUPLICATE] Found and removed {duplicates_found} duplicates ({dup_rate:.1f}%)")
        return self.deduplicated_data
        
    def analyze_dataset_composition(self) -> Dict[str, float]:
        """STEP 4: Analyze composition by source type."""
        composition = defaultdict(int)
        total_examples = len(self.deduplicated_data)
        
        for match in self.deduplicated_data:
            composition[match.source_type] += 1
        
        composition_pct = {
            source: (count / total_examples * 100) 
            for source, count in composition.items()
        }
        
        print(f"[COMPOSITION] Dataset breakdown:")
        for source, pct in composition_pct.items():
            print(f"  {source}: {pct:.1f}%")
        
        return composition_pct
        
    def balance_dataset_by_bias(self
sections.target_distribution:
Dict[str, float]) -> List[TrainingMatch]:
        """STEP 5: Upsample underrepresented bias categories."""
        print(f"[BALANCE] Applying intentional reweighting...")
        
        # Group by bias category
        by_bias = defaultdict(list)
        for match in self.deduplicated_data:
            by_bias[match.bias_category].append(match)
        
        balanced = []
        total_needed = len(self.deduplicated_data)
        
        for bias_cat, weight in target_distribution.items():
            target_count = int(total_needed * weight)
            available = by_bias.get(bias_cat, [])
            
            if len(available) < target_count:
                # Upsample by oversampling
                selected = available * (target_count // len(available) + 1)
                selected = selected[:target_count]
            else:
                # Downsample
                selected = random.sample(available, target_count)
            
            balanced.extend(selected)
            print(f"  {bias_cat}: {len(selected)} examples (target: {target_count})")
        
        self.balanced_data = balanced[:total_needed]
        return self.balanced_data
        
    def generate_dataset_report(self) -> Dict:
        """Generate comprehensive dataset quality report."""
        report = {
            "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat(),
            "stage": "final",
            "raw_examples": len(self.raw_data),
            "cleaned_examples": len(self.cleaned_data),
            "deduplicated_examples": len(self.deduplicated_data),
            "balanced_examples": len(self.balanced_data),
            "average_quality": sum(
                m.quality_score for m in self.balanced_data
            ) / len(self.balanced_data) if self.balanced_data else 0,
            "distribution_by_source": self.analyze_dataset_composition(),
            "bias_distribution": self._get_bias_distribution()
        }
        return report
        
    def _get_bias_distribution(self) -> Dict[str, int]:
        """Calculate bias representation in final dataset."""
        bias_counts = defaultdict(int)
        for match in self.balanced_data:
            bias_counts[match.bias_category] += 1
        return dict(bias_counts)

# ============ EXAMPLE USAGE ============

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Simulate raw training data from cricket match statistics
    raw_matches = [
        # High-quality, diverse cricket players (various nationalities and styles)
        TrainingMatch("match_001", "Virat Kohli", 1, 95, 112, "academic", 0.95, False, False, "none"),
        TrainingMatch("match_002", "Steve Smith", 1, 85, 130, "web_crawl", 0.92, False, False, "geographic"),
        TrainingMatch("match_003", "Jasprit Bumrah", 2, 35, 45, "code", 0.88, False, False, "none"),
        TrainingMatch("match_004", "Ben Stokes", 1, 78, 95, "social_media", 0.85, False, False, "none"),
        TrainingMatch("match_005", "Rohit Sharma", 1, 102, 108, "academic", 0.96, False, False, "none"),
        # Duplicates (same match data repeated)
        TrainingMatch("match_006", "Virat Kohli", 1, 95, 112, "academic", 0.95, False, False, "none"),
        TrainingMatch("match_007", "Virat Kohli", 1, 95, 112, "web_crawl", 0.95, False, False, "none"),
        # Low quality (corrupted or noisy data)
        TrainingMatch("match_008", "Unknown_Player_X", 1, 5, 150, "social_media", 0.25, False, False, "none"),
        # Contains PII (should be removed)
        TrainingMatch("match_009", "player@example.com", 1, 42, 78, "web_crawl", 0.70, False, True, "none"),
        # Underrepresented geographic diversity
        TrainingMatch("match_010", "Pat Cummins", 1, 88, 102, "code", 0.91, False, False, "geographic"),
        TrainingMatch("match_011", "Babar Azam", 1, 94, 110, "academic", 0.93, False, False, "geographic"),
        TrainingMatch("match_012", "Kane Williamson", 1, 96, 115, "social_media", 0.89, False, False, "geographic"),
    ]
    
    # Initialize pipeline
    pipeline = CricketDatasetPipeline()
    pipeline.ingest_raw_data(raw_matches)
    
    print("\n=== DATASET PIPELINE EXECUTION ===")
    print(f"Total cricket players in league: {len(raw_matches)}\n")
    
    # Execute pipeline stages
    pipeline.filter_data_quality(min_quality=0.6)
    print()
    pipeline.deduplicate_data()
    print()
    pipeline.analyze_dataset_composition()
    print()
    
    # Apply intentional reweighting to balance bias
    target_weights = {
        "none": 0.65,
        "geographic": 0.25,
        "gender": 0.10
    }
    pipeline.balance_dataset_by_bias(target_weights)
    print()
    
    # Generate final report
    report = pipeline.generate_dataset_report()
    print(f"\n=== FINAL DATASET REPORT ===")
    print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))

sections.content: This code demonstrates the complete dataset pipeline in LLM training. The CricketDatasetPipeline class models the four critical stages: (1) **Ingestion** loads raw examples from diverse sources (simulated as cricket match statistics); (2) **Filtering** removes PII-containing matches (like 'player@example.com') and low-quality entries with quality_score below 0.6, reducing dataset size by approximately 25% in this example; (3) **Deduplication** identifies matches with identical statistical signatures (Virat Kohli's 95-run innings appears twice) and retains only unique examples, eliminating duplicates common in web-scraped datasets; (4) **Composition Analysis** breaks down the dataset by source type, revealing that raw internet data is typically skewed (web_crawl might dominate), necessitating reweighting. The **bias balancing** stage applies intentional upsampling to underrepresented categories—notice geographic diversity (Steve Smith, Babar Azam, Kane Williamson) is intentionally increased from its natural frequency to 25% through upsampling, correcting for the reality that English-language cricket coverage overweights certain nations. The final report shows that the original 12 examples were reduced to 8 after cleaning (PII removal, quality filtering, deduplication), then rebalanced to ensure adequate representation of different geographic regions. This mirrors how production LLM training reduces raw petabytes of internet text to hundreds of gigabytes of clean, balanced training data. The quality scores (0.25 to 0.96) simulate confidence metrics used in real pipelines, where low-confidence entries are either removed or manually reviewed. The dataset_report method provides versioning information (timestamp) and detailed statistics—exactly what engineers use to diagnose why a deployed model exhibits unexpected biases.
How It Works Under the Hood

At runtime, the dataset pipeline operates as a series of streaming transformations over massive data files, typically organized as sharded binary formats (like TFRecord or Parquet) for efficient parallel processing. When training begins, the data loader spawns multiple worker processes (commonly 32-64 on large clusters) that independently read disjoint shards of the dataset, apply transformations (tokenization, padding, normalization), and feed mini-batches to GPUs. Deduplication requires building in-memory hash tables or approximate membership data structures (like Bloom filters) that track seen sequences, consuming significant memory. For a billion-token dataset with deduplication, storing hash digests alone requires several gigabytes of RAM. Distributed deduplication is non-trivial: workers must coordinate to identify duplicates across shards, typically using a centralized hash service or multi-stage MapReduce-style computation. Quality filtering uses trained classifiers (often smaller neural networks) that score each document, requiring forward passes through the quality model for billions of examples—a process that can take weeks on large-scale datasets. Sampling and reweighting are implemented at the data loader level: rather than pre-computing a reweighted dataset (wasteful), loaders dynamically adjust sampling probabilities per batch. For example, if academic papers should represent 30% of training despite being 5% of the corpus, the loader upsamples academic examples with probability 6x while downsampling web content. This reweighting must be carefully implemented because biased sampling affects gradient statistics: upsampled data contributes larger gradients, which can destabilize training if not properly managed through gradient normalization or learning rate adjustment. The memory footprint of the entire pipeline—loading raw text, applying filters, deduplication, tokenization, and batching—typically reaches 500GB-2TB for large models, distributed across 64-256 machines.
